answer:
In *The Wanderer*, an Old English elegy, the lone wanderer (earth-stepper) speaks of his exile and grief following the death of his lord ("gold-friend") and kinsmen, evoking memories of communal joys in the mead-hall that are now irretrievably lost amid *wyrd* (fate).[^1]

He describes himself as "far from free kinsmen," "winter-sorrowing over the ice-bound waves," "hall-sad," and seeking "one who in the mead-hall my love would know, or would comfort me, the friendless one." He recalls "hall-warriors and the taking of treasure, how in his youth his gold-friend welcomed him to the feast," but laments, "All that joy has perished!" Sorrow renews when "the memory of kinsmen sweeps through his mind; he greets them with joyful words, eagerly scans the host of warriors."[^2]

The poem broadens to mutability: earls "suddenly left the hall-floor," "wine-halls crumble," lords are "deprived of joy," and the speaker rhetorically mourns vanished hall life—"Where have the feast-benches gone? Where are the hall-joys? Alas, the bright cup! Alas, the mailed warrior!"—all swept away by war, beasts, and decay.[^3]

A wise voice concludes by advising restraint in grief and seeking heavenly comfort.[^4]

[^1]: [*Old English Elegies*, ch. 11 (*The Wanderer*), translation](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/).
[^2]: [*Old English Elegies*, ch. 11 (*The Wanderer*), ¶ [p-9462cc](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/#p-9462cc).
[^3]: [*Old English Elegies*, ch. 11 (*The Wanderer*), ¶ [p-9462cc](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/#p-9462cc).
[^4]: [*Old English Elegies*, ch. 11 (*The Wanderer*), ¶ [p-5bdc65](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/11-the-wanderer/translation/#p-5bdc65).
